dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for a fall with back pain at 715 Oak Street.
Hi ma'am, I'm Mark, an EMT with the rescue squad. Are you the one who fell?
Yes, I fell on my back overnight and I can't really move. It hurts so much.
What is your name, and when is your birthday?
Margaret Smith. I was born in July.
What county are we in right now?
We're in Richmond County.
Can you squeeze my fingers with both hands? Good, that's strong, and no deficits here.
Your airway is clear and your breathing seems normal. We're checking your breathing closely, okay?
Pulse is 96.
Respirations at 24.
Blood pressure is 141/94.
O2 saturation is 92 percent.
Blood sugar is 137.
EKG not applicable for now.
Ma'am, can you tell me when you fell, and was anyone else here for a while after?
I fell yesterday and didn't realize it until a few hours later. My wife found me on the floor around 7.
Ma'am, were you on the ground for how long before we got here?
Maybe 3 hours. I hit my head but didn't pass out.
On a scale of 0 to 10, 10 being the worst pain possible, how would you rate your current pain?
It's about an 8 for the back pain. Doesn't stop.
Do you have any medications you're currently taking? Can you name any for us?
Argatroban and Coumadin.
Any medication or food allergies?
I'm allergic to Heparin.
Your eyes are reactive, and your head and neck appear normal. GCS is 15 right now.
The skin appears dry, warm, with mottling and poor turgor.
We noticed some bruising on your arms and legs and tenderness in the lower back.
We're going to put on a cervical collar. You were very still.
Pulse is still at 96.
Respirations are down to 16 now.
Blood pressure is 136/88.
We're going to hook you up to an EKG for a better look at your heart.
And the EKG shows sinus rhythm.
O2 is now at 96 percent.
We will be giving you a general trauma protocol as per our medical control.
IV is already in your left arm.
Can you describe the pain as sharp, dull, throbbing, or something else?
It's like a heavy, throbbing pressure. Not sure when it'll go.
EKG again is still sinus rhythm, no changes.
O2 is now at 98, breathing at 22.
Blood pressure is at 167/111, pulse 97.
The IV remains in place and EKG monitoring is ongoing.
Pulse is 99, blood pressure 136/92, still 22 breaths a minute.
Per your request, we will transport you to VCU Medical Center. You can change your mind at any time.
We're going to move you onto the stretcher safely. Four of us will carry you.
Now that you're on the stretcher, we'll secure you and take you to the transfer point.
Ready when you are. Let's go.
Ma'am, we see in your records that you had a heart bypass, aortic aneurysm, and lung cancer. Also, a history of Heparin-induced Thrombocytopenia.
I've had CABG and the aneurysm still lingers. The cancer's back too.
And you have hypertension and hyperlipidemia as well.
IV is stable. We'll monitor it closely on the ride.
Looking at your neck for swelling or deformity. You seem okay there.
We did see some bruising, but no skin discoloration otherwise.
IV is still patent. Verified during transport.
We're rolling you over using a standard stretcher. This is part of our safe procedure.
You are now secured. We will carry you to the transfer point outside the house to load the ambulance.
We've started a 4-lead EKG monitoring per protocol. The heart rhythm is currently stable.
The 4-lead EKG continues to show sinus rhythm.
IV is stable. The EKG is still in sync for your heart rhythm.
IV is stable. We've given some verbal reassurance about your condition while we transport.
SpO2 still at 98. Pulse is 99. Blood pressure has returned to 136/92.
We will be contacting the ER team so they can prepare for your arrival with all medical notes and vitals known so far.
Would you like anything on the trip-extra cushion, something for the pain?.
A cushion would help. I just want to get outta this spot.
We've adjusted the stretcher position for better comfort. No reason to worry now if all readings stay normal.
Is the pain slightly better? Still at an 8 or has it changed since the last check?
Still at an 8, but I feel a little better. Thanks.
The 4-lead EKG continues to show no changes in electrical activity, and no cardiac interventions are required at the moment.
Continuing to the hospital. VCU is about ten minutes away now with no incidents so far.
Breathing has improved slightly. O2 still at 98. Falling within normal ranges of oxygenation now showing only minor stress on organs.
Ma'am, did you take any drugs or have food before the fall yesterday?.
Last food or drink, if you remember?.
I had soup with water a few hours before bed Wednesday night.。
Your skin is still warm and dry, and no signs of a pressure wound from prolonged lying, Ms. Smith.
Any nausea or other new symptoms we should monitor? Are you feeling light-headed right now or somewhere else painful?.
No, but I'm tired. Just lay me still and don't bother me further on the way in.
Rest easy. Keep the IV and EKG running to monitor continuously. Let me know if anything changes temporarily or becomes worrisome down the line.
Let me recheck your oxygen levels again as we near the hospital transfer zone before arrival.
All metrics are okay. Ready to bring into the hospital entrance areas now for loading.
Upon arriving at VCU Medical Center, the nurse will be waiting in EDR Room 2 for your arrival when the ambulance arriving works with hospital rules for transfers.
We'll assist you from our stretcher to the hospital bed via a transfer sheet unless stretcher can fit into the room otherwise.
Important notes on report form: allergic to Heparin, Stage IV Lung Cancer, Coumadin and Argatroban use, plus CABG and Heparin-induced Thrombocytopenia history ready to fill in
EMS report includes all work done, like procedure from protocol 4-1, ample note collection, and listing of vitals before and now again
Thank you for being still and working with us. You're in good hands now at the hospital for more detailed assessment and emergency care.
The ambulance is already moving into the hospital area without issues for you to stabilize further so your outcome scores can improve.
We have Ms. Smith from Unit 7, transported to VCU Medical Center as a 67-year-old female with a fall and significant back trauma for full ER intervention now.
The 4-lead EKG monitoring is functioning correctly, with no changes to cardiac rhythm.
You were still alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event throughout our entire transport assessment here in the field.
